name: batch_0000
description: The People's Video is a large-scale, commercially licensed multimodal video dataset of 847K source videos comprising 70.6M clips and 219K hours of content. Every video carries a creator-declared Creative Commons license (CC0 1.0 Universal or CC-BY 4.0 International) verified at collection time via YouTube's metadata API. Clips were produced via PySceneDetect content-aware scene segmentation at 720P resolution, with an average clip length of 11.2 seconds. Detailed natural-language captions (132 words per clip on average) were generated using Qwen3-VL (235B parameters) deployed through the ScalarLM distributed inference platform (vLLM + Megatron-LM) over approximately 2.1M GPU-hours. This batch (batch_0000) is one shard of the larger release. The corpus targets text-to-video generation, video-language pre-training, video captioning, video question answering, temporal grounding, and long-form video understanding.
citeAs: Xaio, B., Kanter, D., Diamos, G. The People's Video: Exploring Commercially Licensed Multimodal Data. 2025.
rai:dataCollection: Source videos were acquired by a custom scraper interfacing with the YouTube Data API v3. The scraper used the search.list endpoint with the videoLicense=creativeCommon filter and additionally restricted results via the videos.list endpoint to videos whose creator-declared license tag was CC0 1.0 Universal or CC-BY 4.0 International. Candidate search results were reviewed by human curators in batches to filter low-quality uploads (static slideshows, corrupted files, near-duplicate re-uploads) and to ensure representation across four primary domain clusters: technical/professional content, educational/instructional content, cultural/performative content, and everyday/environmental content. Acquired videos were segmented into clips with PySceneDetect's ContentDetector and split with FFmpeg without re-encoding, preserving 720P resolution. Frames were sampled at 30-second intervals and grouped into sequential pairs, then captioned by Qwen3-VL (235B parameters) through ScalarLM-orchestrated distributed inference. Each caption record retains full provenance metadata: video identifier, source URL, original description, and temporal sampling parameters.
rai:dataLimitations: The dataset has several known limitations. (1) Licensing scope: while every video carries a creator-declared CC0 or CC-BY license tag, the interaction between per-video Creative Commons declarations and YouTube's platform-level Terms of Service has not been adjudicated, so users in commercial settings should consult independent legal counsel. (2) License stability: YouTube creators may change a video's license after collection; the pipeline records the license tag at acquisition time but does not yet implement continuous re-verification. (3) Sparse temporal sampling: frames are extracted at 30-second intervals, so most clips (avg 11.2s, 37% under 2s) are captioned from one or two frames, limiting fine-grained temporal description. (4) Holistic captions: captions describe each clip holistically rather than moment-by-moment, yielding only marginal within-clip temporal structure (argmax accuracy 34.6% vs 33.3% chance) and limiting direct use for temporal grounding training. (5) Audio stripped: PySceneDetect-cut scene segments discard the source audio track, precluding audio-grounded evaluation without re-fetching source videos. (6) Synthetic captions: captions are model-generated and, despite verification-oriented prompting, will contain some rate of hallucinated or inaccurate descriptions; no fully human-verified caption subset is provided. (7) Long-form coverage: clips exceeding 20 seconds account for only 10% of the collection, limiting utility for tasks requiring extended temporal reasoning. (8) Reproducibility-scale evaluation: experimental results in the accompanying paper are computed on an 8,522-clip validation subset using no-training protocols (CLIP ViT-B/32 retrieval, frozen-encoder adapters, VLM-as-judge); they characterize properties of the released captions and clips, not full-scale downstream training accuracy.
rai:dataBiases: The dataset reflects the demographics, languages, geographies, and production styles of YouTube creators who elect to publish under Creative Commons licenses, which is a non-uniform subset of all video content. Measured distributional profiling on a sampled subset (using CLIP-based forced-choice classifiers, which carry their own biases under visual ambiguity) found a skew toward apparent-male presenters (63.3% on the People's Video sample, with 12.7% apparent-female and the remainder no-person-visible) and a concentration in the apparent-middle-aged bucket (~53–57%), consistent with the prevalence of seated-presenter content (podcasts, interviews, lectures). The taxonomy-level coverage analysis identified sports and culinary categories as comparatively under-represented relative to unconstrained web-crawled corpora. Content is predominantly English-language, since CC-licensed videos surfaced by the curation queries skew Anglophone. Geographic coverage is non-uniform and correlates with the regions where CC licensing is most adopted by creators. Users training models on this corpus should account for these distributional skews and supplement with additional sources where balanced demographic, geographic, or activity coverage is required.
rai:personalSensitiveInformation: Source videos were filmed and published by their creators on YouTube under Creative Commons licenses (CC0 or CC-BY), which grant permission for redistribution and derivative use. Videos may depict identifiable individuals — including the publishing creator, invited speakers, performers, students, and incidental bystanders captured in public or semi-public settings — and may include their voices in the original audio (note: audio is stripped from released scene segments). Video metadata retained alongside each clip includes the publishing channel identifier, video title, original description, upload date, and view count, which can identify the publishing creator. The dataset does not contain explicit categories of sensitive personal data such as government identifiers, financial account numbers, medical records, or precise residential addresses, but content domains include medical-training simulations and educational settings where individuals appear in semi-professional contexts. No automated face de-identification, voice scrubbing, or PII redaction is applied; downstream users responsible for compliance with privacy regulations (e.g., GDPR, CCPA) should perform their own redaction or filtering as required by their jurisdiction and use case.
rai:dataUseCases: Intended uses include: training and evaluation of text-to-video generation models (e.g., diffusion-based architectures); video-language contrastive pre-training; long-form caption generation for video diffusion model conditioning; video captioning and dense video captioning; video question answering; temporal action localization and grounding (with the caveat that the released captions are holistic rather than moment-localized); action recognition on short clips; long-form video understanding on the 10s+ tier; multimodal large language model pre-training; and study of how licensing-aware curation affects model capability and bias profiles. The commercially permissive licensing makes the dataset suitable for both academic research and commercial product development. Out-of-scope uses include any application that would identify, surveil, or otherwise harm the individuals depicted in the videos, biometric identification, generation of non-consensual likenesses, and any use that violates applicable law or the original Creative Commons attribution requirements.
rai:dataSocialImpact: The dataset is designed to address two converging pressures on the video-AI research community. First, the rising wave of copyright litigation surrounding AI training data has made licensing provenance a first-class concern; by providing per-video Creative Commons documentation, this dataset offers the auditability that legal and compliance teams increasingly require and lowers the barrier for researchers and small organizations who cannot otherwise absorb the legal risk of training on indiscriminately scraped corpora. Second, the broader scarcity of high-quality public training data — particularly in video, where storage and processing costs amplify the gap — threatens to concentrate frontier capability in the hands of organizations with access to proprietary corpora; releasing a large-scale, commercially usable alternative helps democratize access. Potential negative impacts include: (a) the dataset's demographic and geographic skews could be amplified by models trained on it without corrective measures; (b) commercially-deployable video generation models trained on this data could be misused for non-consensual imagery, deepfakes, or disinformation, despite the depicted individuals having consented to CC distribution of the source video and not to derivative generative use; (c) attribution requirements under CC-BY are inherited by downstream models in legally untested ways, which may shift compliance burden onto deployers. Mitigations include published limitations and bias documentation, encouragement of supplementary curation for under-represented categories, and the recommendation that derivative works adopt comparable provenance documentation.

prov:wasGeneratedBy.name: The People's Video data collection and captioning pipeline
prov:wasGeneratedBy.description: Multi-stage pipeline composed of: (1) license-aware acquisition via a custom scraper over the YouTube Data API v3 (search.list with videoLicense=creativeCommon, then videos.list to verify CC0 1.0 or CC-BY 4.0 declared license); (2) human curation review of candidate query batches across a four-cluster domain taxonomy; (3) content-aware scene segmentation with PySceneDetect's ContentDetector and FFmpeg-based clip cutting without re-encoding at 720P; (4) frame extraction at 30-second intervals saved as lossless PNGs keyed by md5(source URL); (5) batched paragraph-form captioning with Qwen3-VL (235B parameters) deployed through ScalarLM, which integrates vLLM (PagedAttention) for inference and Megatron-LM for tensor/pipeline parallelism, scheduled on Kubernetes; (6) caption persistence with full provenance metadata and md5-keyed deduplication for restart safety; (7) sharded upload to the Hugging Face Hub. The full captioning workload consumed approximately 2.1M GPU-hours.
prov:wasGeneratedBy.citation: Xaio, B., Kanter, D., Diamos, G. The People's Video: Exploring Commercially Licensed Multimodal Data. 2025.
